title: How to Negotiate a Higher Rate with a New Client

interlinking_suggestions.title: How to Calculate Your Billable Rate as a Freelancer
How to Write a Contract for a UK-Based Client

author.name: Chloé Dubois
author.bio: Chloé is a communications expert who coaches freelancers on the art of client management. She writes about negotiation, project management, and building long-term, high-value client relationships.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Reframe your initial client call from a sales pitch to a 'Value Discovery' audit to anchor your fee against the business problem's true cost, not your time.
When facing price pushback, deploy the 'Acknowledge, Re-anchor, Offer' framework to pivot the discussion from your rate to the client's control over the project scope.
Prevent profit erosion by immediately codifying a verbal 'yes' into a contract that specifies deliverables, exclusions, payment terms, and a formal Change Order process.
For global engagements, dictate payment terms in a stable currency (USD/EUR) and explicitly assign responsibility for transfer fees and VAT to protect your bottom line.
summary: Independent professionals often treat rate negotiation as a fearful confrontation, leaving their business vulnerable to devalued brand perception, profit erosion, and weak agreements. The core advice is to implement a three-stage Client Intake Protocol: frame value before discussing price, present your rate as a data-driven business case, and fortify the agreement with a clear contract. This systematic approach replaces negotiation anxiety with professional confidence, empowering you to command premium rates and build secure, profitable client partnerships.
ai_overview_answer: To effectively command premium rates, independent professionals should adopt a structured, three-stage Client Intake Protocol that replaces negotiation with a process-driven approach. This system begins by framing your value *before* discussing price, using a "Value Discovery" audit to understand the client's core business problem and its financial implications. Next, present your proposal as a data-driven business case that connects your fee directly to the client's return on investment and risk mitigation. If you receive pushback, use an "Acknowledge, Re-anchor, Offer" framework to guide the conversation toward adjusting the scope, not your rate. After gaining verbal agreement, fortify the deal with a contract that codifies the scope, lists specific exclusions, and defines clear payment terms. Finally, implement a formal Change Order process to ensure any additional work is properly scoped and compensated. This protocol transforms the engagement from a price haggle into a strategic partnership you lead.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

@graph.headline: How to Negotiate a Higher Rate with a New Client
@graph.description: This article provides a comprehensive Client Intake Protocol, a three-stage framework to give you control over the client communication and rate-setting process, empowering you to justify your value and fortify every new relationship.

step.name: Stage 1: The Pre-Negotiation Protocol: Frame Value Before You Talk Numbers

itemListElement.name: Conduct a 'Value Discovery' Audit, Not a Sales Call
itemListElement.text: Lead a diagnostic session focused entirely on the client's business to uncover the true, quantifiable business pain. Use strategic questioning to unearth the financial implications of their problem, shifting the conversation from your cost to their investment.

itemListElement.name: Develop a 'Risk Reversal' Statement
itemListElement.text: Reframe your premium rate as the client's protection against costly mistakes. Formulate a clear statement that articulates how you mitigate their primary risks, demonstrating that the real risk isn't hiring you, but proceeding without you.

itemListElement.name: Anchor Your Authority with a Professional Rate Card
itemListElement.text: Use a rate card as a strategic document to position yourself as an established business. It anchors your value high, sets clear expectations, and provides a framework for the entire financial conversation.

step.name: Stage 2: Executing the Negotiation: A Data-Driven Exchange, Not a Confrontation

itemListElement.name: Present Your Proposal as a Business Case
itemListElement.text: Your proposal should be a strategic document that explicitly connects every dollar of your fee to the outcomes they desire and the risks you mitigate. Frame your rate as an investment designed to achieve a specific, quantified result.

itemListElement.name: Deploy the 'Acknowledge, Re-anchor, Offer' Framework for Pushback
itemListElement.text: When a client questions your rate, use a three-step protocol: Acknowledge their concern, re-anchor the conversation to the value you co-discovered, and offer a choice to adjust the scope, not your rate.

itemListElement.name: Use Tiered Options to Give Control and Secure the Win
itemListElement.text: Present two or three distinct packages to reframe the client's decision from a 'yes or no' to 'which one is best for us?' This keeps you in control of the financial floor while giving them agency.

itemListElement.name: Ground Your Value in Expertise
itemListElement.text: Position your fee as a direct reflection of your expertise and its impact. You are not charging for your time; you are charging for the value you create.

step.name: Stage 3: Fortifying the Deal: The Negotiation Isn't Over When They Say 'Yes'

itemListElement.name: Codify the 'Scope & Exclusions' Clause Immediately
itemListElement.text: Finalize the agreement with a contract that explicitly states what is included and, crucially, what is excluded. Define quantifiable deliverables, revision limits, and communication channels to create a firm boundary around the work.

itemListElement.name: Define 'Payment Milestones & Net Terms' with Cross-Border Clarity
itemListElement.text: Remove all ambiguity from the payment process. Your contract must specify the payment schedule, method, responsibility for fees, and net terms to ensure you are paid on time and in full.

itemListElement.name: Establish a Formal Change Order Process
itemListElement.text: Include a clause that outlines the exact procedure for handling any request that falls outside the agreed-upon scope. This transforms potential conflict into a simple business transaction, ensuring you are compensated for all your work.

mainEntity.name: How do I respond professionally when a client says my rate is too high?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Use the three-step **"Acknowledge, Re-anchor, Offer"** framework. This isn't about defending your price; it's about guiding the conversation back to value.
1.  **Acknowledge:** "I understand that budget is a primary consideration for this project."
2.  **Re-anchor:** "To achieve the accelerated timeline we discussed and mitigate the compliance risks, the project requires a senior level of expertise, which is what this rate ensures."
3.  **Offer:** "If the budget is firm, we can adjust the scope. We could remove [Specific Deliverable] or extend the timeline to align. Which of those paths would you prefer to explore?" This frames you as a flexible problem-solver, not a haggling vendor.

mainEntity.name: What should an email template for a rate proposal include?

acceptedAnswer.text:
A powerful template allows you to build a compelling case quickly with these core components:
*   **The Business Case Justification:** A concise paragraph restating the client's primary goal and connecting your proposed rate to the ROI, risk mitigation, or efficiency gains you will deliver.
*   **Optional Value Clauses:** Pre-written sentences for specific situations, like a rush timeline ("This rate accounts for the reprioritization of other client work to meet your accelerated deadline") or specialized expertise ("This project requires specific expertise in [Your Niche], which is factored into this value-based rate").
*   **Clear, Scope-Based Next Steps:** End by reiterating the choice. "If the proposed scope and rate are aligned, I'm ready to move forward. If the budget is a firm constraint, I'm happy to discuss adjusting the scope to meet it."

mainEntity.name: How can I justify a higher rate to a large corporate client?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Build a business case using their language: data, ROI, and risk management. Instead of talking about your hours, quantify your value in terms they care about.
*   **Focus on ROI:** "The investment for this project is [Your Rate]. By streamlining this process, we project a time savings of 20 hours per month for your team, which translates to roughly [$X] in recovered productivity annually."
*   **Highlight Risk Mitigation:** "My fee includes a rigorous QA process that prevents the costly compliance errors common in this type of rollout, which have previously cost firms in your sector an average of [$Y] to remediate."
*   **Emphasize Efficiency Gains:** Show how your work helps them achieve more with less. By focusing on tangible business impact, your rate becomes a logical investment.

mainEntity.name: What's the best way to create a rate card for international clients?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Structure it like a professional services firm to signal you are a serious, global business entity. A well-designed rate card removes ambiguity and positions you as an established expert.
*   **List Service Tiers:** Offer clear packages (e.g., "Strategic Audit," "Full Implementation," "Ongoing Retainer") with defined deliverables.
*   **Specify Currency:** Clearly state that all rates are in a stable currency like USD or EUR.
*   **Include Standard Terms:** Add your non-negotiable payment terms directly on the card. For example: *"All rates are in USD. Payment via Wise. Net-15 terms. A 50% non-refundable deposit is required to commence all new projects."* This pre-sets expectations before a conversation begins.

mainEntity.name: Should I let the client name their budget first?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Yes, this can be a powerful strategic move during your Stage 1 "Value Discovery" audit. Posing the question, *"What budget have you allocated to solve this challenge?"* is a diagnostic tool.
*   **If the budget is high:** It validates your premium positioning and gives you a clear path to present your top-tier service.
*   **If the budget is low:** It's an immediate red flag that saves you time. You can instantly qualify them out or explain *why* a successful outcome requires a more significant investment, turning their low number into a stepping stone for a value-based conversation.

mainEntity.name: How do I factor in currency risk or international compliance costs?

acceptedAnswer.text: Build these costs directly into your baseline rate as operational overhead. Your rate isn't just your time; it's the cost of running a global business. Add a buffer of 3-5% to your calculated rate to account for potential currency fluctuations and administrative time. Do not itemize this for the client. A line item for "currency risk" undermines your authority. It is an internal calculation that ensures your profitability, allowing you to present a single, confident, all-inclusive project rate.

body.content: Stop Negotiating. Start Leading: A Protocol for Your Business-of-One

You are not a freelancer asking for a raise; you are the CEO of a global business entering a strategic partnership. This mindset is the foundation of your success. Yet, conventional advice on negotiating rates treats you like a nervous employee, focusing on courage rather than process. This conversation isn't about overcoming fear—it’s about strategically mitigating risk. The anxiety you feel isn't just about naming a higher price. It’s a rational fear of the unknown, a series of "what ifs" that can erode a new engagement before it begins.
The legitimate business risks keeping you from a premium pricing strategy are:

items: **Brand Devaluation:** What if accepting a lower rate signals to this client—and the wider market—that your expertise is a commodity? This single decision can set a precedent that is difficult to reverse.

**Profit Erosion:** What if the initial scope inevitably expands? Without a fortified agreement, every additional request and "quick call" destroys your profit margin, turning a premium project into a pro-bono nightmare.

**Weak Foundations:** What if the engagement is built on a flimsy verbal agreement, leaving you exposed to payment delays, communication breakdowns, and a fundamental lack of respect for your expertise?

body.content: These are not personal insecurities; they are critical business threats. Forget disconnected "tips and tricks." To command the rates you deserve and protect your business, you need a repeatable, defensible system. This article provides that system: a comprehensive **Client Intake Protocol**. This three-stage framework gives you control over the client communication and rate-setting process from the first interaction, empowering you to justify your value, fortify every new relationship, and ensure you are not just well-paid, but thoroughly protected.
Stage 1: The Pre-Negotiation Protocol: Frame Value Before You Talk Numbers

A defensible system begins not with a number, but with a reframing of your first conversation. To command a premium rate, you must seize control of the narrative *before* pricing is ever discussed. This initial stage establishes you as a strategic partner, not a vendor awaiting a budget. You will shift the focus from what you cost to the value you create, making your premium rate the logical conclusion. This isn't a sales tactic; it's a diagnostic business process.

items:
**Conduct a "Value Discovery" Audit, Not a Sales Call**
Your first interaction sets the tone for the entire engagement. Resist the urge to present credentials or pitch services. Instead, lead a diagnostic session focused entirely on the client's business. Your goal is to move beyond the surface-level request and uncover the true, quantifiable business pain. This audit transforms the conversation from your cost to their investment. Use strategic questioning to unearth the financial implications of their problem.
*   **Strategic Question Examples:**
    *   *"What is the opportunity cost if this project is delayed by another quarter?"* This forces the client to calculate the value of lost revenue or missed market advantage, anchoring your future fee against a much larger number.
    *   *"What would a 10% efficiency gain in this area mean for your team's annual output?"* This connects your work directly to productivity and profitability.
    *   *"Beyond the immediate deliverables, what is the strategic importance of this project's success to your career or department goals?"* This uncovers the personal and political stakes, which are often powerful motivators.

**Develop a "Risk Reversal" Statement**
A premium rate can feel like a risk to a new client. Your job is to reframe it as their greatest protection. Your expertise is an insurance policy against costly mistakes, compliance failures, brand damage, and project delays. Before discussing price, formulate a clear statement that articulates how you mitigate their primary risks. This demonstrates that the *real* risk isn't hiring you, but proceeding without you.
*   **Risk Reversal Examples:**
    *   For a compliance-heavy project: *"My process includes a multi-stage regulatory review that prevents the costly compliance errors that often derail projects in this sector, saving clients significant legal and financial exposure."*
    *   For a complex tech build: *"Many projects like this are vulnerable to scope creep that inflates timelines and budgets. My agile development framework and bi-weekly sprint reviews ensure we stay on track and within the agreed scope."

**Anchor Your Authority with a Professional Rate Card**
Before a formal proposal, you need a tool that establishes your value and professional standing. A rate card is not just a list of prices; it's a strategic document that positions you as an established business, not a gig worker haggling for a fee. It anchors your value high, sets clear expectations, and provides a framework for the entire financial conversation—a powerful signal of confidence and experience.

rows: **Strategic Partnership**
Long-term, complex initiatives requiring deep integration.
Retained advisory, dedicated capacity, full scope management.
Monthly Retainer (USD/EUR)
**Core Project Package**
Defined projects with clear start/end dates and deliverables.
End-to-end project execution, milestone-based delivery.
Fixed Project Fee (50% Upfront)
**Diagnostic & Audit**
Initial-phase engagements to define strategy and scope.
In-depth analysis, strategic roadmap, risk assessment.
Fixed Fee (100% Upfront)

body.content: Stage 2: Executing the Negotiation: A Data-Driven Exchange, Not a Confrontation

With your value firmly anchored, you shift from preparation to execution. This stage is not a confrontation; it is a collaborative, data-driven exchange where you guide the client toward a logical investment decision. Your confidence comes from the diagnostic work completed in Stage 1. You are no longer a service provider—you are a business partner presenting a clear case for a specific return on investment. This is the essence of a successful pricing strategy: turning a subjective conversation about cost into an objective decision about value.

items:
**Present Your Proposal as a Business Case**
Never email a number in isolation. Your proposal is a strategic document, a direct reflection of the "Value Discovery" audit. It must function as a business case, explicitly connecting every dollar of your fee to the outcomes they desire and the risks you mitigate. Frame your rate as an investment designed to achieve a specific, quantified result. For instance: *"The total project investment is [Your Rate]. This is structured to capture the [$XXX,XXX] in new market opportunity we identified and to prevent the estimated [$XX,XXX] in compliance penalties associated with an improper rollout."* This language transforms your fee from an expense into a calculated financial tool.

**Deploy the "Acknowledge, Re-anchor, Offer" Framework for Pushback**
When a client says your rate is high, it is a predictable part of the process, not a crisis. Avoid the defensive urge to discount your value. Instead, execute a calm, three-step protocol that maintains your authority and keeps the communication productive.
1.  **Acknowledge:** Start with empathy to validate their concern. "I understand that this is a significant investment and that budget is a key consideration." This simple statement de-escalates tension and shows you are listening.
2.  **Re-anchor:** Immediately pivot back to the value you co-discovered. "Based on our conversation, the scope required to achieve [Specific Outcome, e.g., 'a 10% increase in lead conversion'] and mitigate [Specific Risk, e.g., 'brand damage from a poor launch'] is reflected in this investment."
3.  **Offer a Choice:** Empower the client by putting them in control of the variable—scope, not your rate. "If the budget is fixed, we can absolutely work within that. We would need to adjust the scope to match. For example, we could forgo [Deliverable X] or reduce [Service Y]. Which of those paths would you prefer to explore?"

**Use Tiered Options to Give Control and Secure the Win**
A powerful technique is to present tiered options. Instead of a single, take-it-or-leave-it price, structure your business case around two or three distinct packages. This reframes the client's decision from a stressful "yes or no" to a collaborative "which one is best for us?" You remain in control of the financial floor while giving them agency.

rows: **Option 1: Premium**
The "All-Inclusive" or "White Glove" service with added strategic value.
Anchors your value high and makes the middle option seem more reasonable.
**Option 2: Recommended**
The "Core Engagement" that perfectly matches the scope discussed.
This is your target proposal and the one you expect them to choose.
**Option 3: Reduced Scope**
A "Foundational" or "Essential" version with fewer deliverables.
Ensures you can still win the engagement even with a firm budget constraint.

items:
**Ground Your Value in Expertise**
Ultimately, this entire process is about positioning your fee as a direct reflection of your expertise and its impact. As world-renowned consultant Alan Weiss advises, this is the core of value-based pricing: *"My fee is based on my contribution to the value you've stipulated you'll be receiving, providing an excellent ROI for you and equitable compensation for me."* Adopt this mindset. You are not charging for your time; you are charging for the value you create. When you can confidently articulate that value, the number becomes a logical and fair conclusion.

body.content: Stage 3: Fortifying the Deal: The Negotiation Isn't Over When They Say 'Yes'

When a client agrees to your rate, it is a milestone, not the finish line. The verbal "yes" is fragile, exposed to misinterpretation, shifting expectations, and delayed payments. Your final leadership act is to move from verbal agreement to contractual clarity. This isn't adversarial legal maneuvering; it's professional stewardship. You are protecting the value you both just agreed upon by building a clear, resilient framework for the partnership.

items:
**Codify the "Scope & Exclusions" Clause Immediately**
The single greatest threat to your profitability is scope creep. Your agreement isn't finalized until the contract explicitly states not only what is included, but also what is *excluded*. This creates a firm boundary around the work. Go beyond listing deliverables; define the process.

Your "Scope of Services" should detail:
*   **Quantifiable Deliverables:** "Delivery of one 2,500-word market analysis report in .docx format."
*   **Revision Limits:** "Includes two rounds of consolidated feedback."
*   **Meeting Allowances:** "Four 45-minute project status meetings via video conference."
*   **Communication Channels:** "All project-related communication will be conducted within our shared project management tool."

The "Exclusions" section is just as crucial. Be explicit about what is *not* included to manage expectations: "This scope excludes the creation of presentation slides, social media assets, or any raw data files."

**Define "Payment Milestones & Net Terms" with Cross-Border Clarity**
A negotiated rate is meaningless if it isn't paid on time and in full. Your contract must remove all ambiguity from the payment process, especially for global clients. Dictate the terms.

rows: **Payment Schedule**
The exact timing of each payment.
"50% of the total project fee is due upon signing to commence work; 50% is due upon final delivery."
**Payment Method**
How you will be paid.
"All payments to be made in USD via Wise transfer."
**Responsibility for Fees**
Who covers transaction costs.
"Client is responsible for all bank transfer and processing fees."
**Net Terms**
The window for payment after invoicing.
"Payment is due Net-15 from the date of invoice receipt."
**VAT Handling (for EU)**
How Value Added Tax is managed.
"Client is responsible for VAT via the Reverse-Charge mechanism."

body.content: The Reverse-Charge mechanism is a simplification for B2B services within the EU (and often with non-EU providers) that shifts the responsibility for reporting VAT from you to the client, a significant administrative relief.

items:
**Establish a Formal Change Order Process**
Anticipate that changes will happen. A formal Change Order process transforms potential conflict into a simple business transaction. This clause outlines the exact procedure for handling any request that falls outside the agreed-upon scope, ensuring you are compensated for all your work.

Your clause should state that:
1.  Any request for work outside the defined scope must be submitted in writing.
2.  You will provide a written "Change Order" detailing the additional work, the associated cost, and any timeline adjustments.
3.  Work on the additional request will begin only *after* the client has provided written approval of the Change Order.

body.content: This protocol maintains your role as the strategic partner. It prevents scope creep, reinforces the value of your work, and keeps the engagement on a foundation of mutual respect.

A formal protocol transforms a tense back-and-forth into a managed business process, but even the best system invites questions. View these common scenarios not as obstacles, but as opportunities to reinforce your value and lead the client toward a successful partnership.
Frequently Asked Questions

items:
**How do I respond professionally when a client says my rate is too high?**
Use the three-step **"Acknowledge, Re-anchor, Offer"** framework. This isn't about defending your price; it's about guiding the conversation back to value.
1.  **Acknowledge:** "I understand that budget is a primary consideration for this project."
2.  **Re-anchor:** "To achieve the accelerated timeline we discussed and mitigate the compliance risks, the project requires a senior level of expertise, which is what this rate ensures."
3.  **Offer:** "If the budget is firm, we can adjust the scope. We could remove [Specific Deliverable] or extend the timeline to align. Which of those paths would you prefer to explore?" This frames you as a flexible problem-solver, not a haggling vendor.

**What should an email template for a rate proposal include?**
Think modular, not monolithic. A powerful template allows you to build a compelling case quickly with these core components:
*   **The Business Case Justification:** A concise paragraph restating the client's primary goal and connecting your proposed rate to the ROI, risk mitigation, or efficiency gains you will deliver.
*   **Optional Value Clauses:** Pre-written sentences for specific situations, like a rush timeline ("This rate accounts for the reprioritization of other client work to meet your accelerated deadline") or specialized expertise ("This project requires specific expertise in [Your Niche], which is factored into this value-based rate").
*   **Clear, Scope-Based Next Steps:** End by reiterating the choice. "If the proposed scope and rate are aligned, I'm ready to move forward. If the budget is a firm constraint, I'm happy to discuss adjusting the scope to meet it."

**How can I justify a higher rate to a large corporate client?**
Build a business case using their language: data, ROI, and risk management. Instead of talking about your hours, quantify your value in terms they care about.
*   **Focus on ROI:** "The investment for this project is [Your Rate]. By streamlining this process, we project a time savings of 20 hours per month for your team, which translates to roughly [$X] in recovered productivity annually."
*   **Highlight Risk Mitigation:** "My fee includes a rigorous QA process that prevents the costly compliance errors common in this type of rollout, which have previously cost firms in your sector an average of [$Y] to remediate."
*   **Emphasize Efficiency Gains:** Show how your work helps them achieve more with less. By focusing on tangible business impact, your rate becomes a logical investment.

**What's the best way to create a rate card for international clients?**
Structure it like a professional services firm to signal you are a serious, global business entity. A well-designed rate card removes ambiguity and positions you as an established expert.
*   **List Service Tiers:** Offer clear packages (e.g., "Strategic Audit," "Full Implementation," "Ongoing Retainer") with defined deliverables.
*   **Specify Currency:** Clearly state that all rates are in a stable currency like USD or EUR.
*   **Include Standard Terms:** Add your non-negotiable payment terms directly on the card. For example: *"All rates are in USD. Payment via Wise. Net-15 terms. A 50% non-refundable deposit is required to commence all new projects."* This pre-sets expectations before a conversation begins.

**Should I let the client name their budget first?**
Yes, this can be a powerful strategic move during your Stage 1 "Value Discovery" audit. Posing the question, *"What budget have you allocated to solve this challenge?"* is a diagnostic tool.
*   **If the budget is high:** It validates your premium positioning and gives you a clear path to present your top-tier service.
*   **If the budget is low:** It's an immediate red flag that saves you time. You can instantly qualify them out or explain *why* a successful outcome requires a more significant investment, turning their low number into a stepping stone for a value-based conversation.

**How do I factor in currency risk or international compliance costs?**
Build these costs directly into your baseline rate as operational overhead. Your rate isn't just your time; it's the cost of running a global business. Add a buffer of 3-5% to your calculated rate to account for potential currency fluctuations and administrative time. Do not itemize this for the client. A line item for "currency risk" undermines your authority. It is an internal calculation that ensures your profitability, allowing you to present a single, confident, all-inclusive project rate.

body.content: From Negotiation to Partnership: Implementing Your Protocol

Factoring in currency risk or crafting the perfect email are crucial tactics, but they are components of a more powerful strategic system. The persistent anxiety many independent professionals feel around money comes from the lack of a system. When you replace improvisation with a professional protocol, the fear of negotiation is replaced by the quiet confidence of a process you control.
This framework is a deliberate progression that transforms a confrontational moment into a collaborative engagement where you are the expert guide.

items: **Stage 1: Framing Value** shifts the dynamic before a number is ever mentioned. You move the conversation away from your cost and anchor it firmly to the client's business outcomes.
**Stage 2: Executing the Exchange** brings your pricing strategy to life. You present a business case, not a price list, where your fee is the logical conclusion of the value you’ve established.
**Stage 3: Fortifying the Deal** separates amateurs from professionals. You codify the agreement with clear contractual boundaries that protect your profitability and mitigate future risk.

body.content: By systematically moving through these phases, the entire exercise is reframed. You are no longer a vendor defending a price; you are a strategic partner guiding an investment decision. The focus moves from what you cost to what you are worth.

rows: Hopes the client accepts the rate.
Leads the client to a logical conclusion about value.
Defends their price when questioned.
Re-anchors the conversation to the client's ROI.
Fears scope creep and late payments.
Proactively defines boundaries and terms in the contract.
Focuses on getting the initial "yes."
Focuses on building a secure, profitable partnership.

body.content: Ultimately, this isn't just about earning more on the next project. It's about building a more resilient, profitable, and secure Business-of-One. This protocol is your standard operating procedure for client intake, a system designed to filter for the best clients and establish the terms for your best work. Adapt this framework to your voice and make it your own.
You are the CEO—it's time to lead the intake process like one.
